content.problemStatement:
Let's say a number $x$ appears $f[x]$ times in the array $A$. \
In how many ways can you form a permutation subsequence of size $L$?

Option 1: $\prod_{i=1}^L (f[i] + 1)$ 

We are increasing the frequency of each element by 1 and then multiplying it with the frequencies of the other elements, which are also increased by 1.

Option 2: $\prod_{i=1}^L f[i]$ 

We are taking the frequency of each element and then multiplying it with the frequencies of the other elements.

Option 3: $1 + \prod_{i=1}^L f[i]$ 

We are taking the frequency of each element, multiplying it with the frequencies of the other elements, and adding 1 at last.

Option 4: $\sum_{i=1}^L f[i]$ 

We are adding the frequency of all the element.
constraints:
- $1 \leq T \leq 1000$
- $2 \leq N \leq 10^5$
- $1 \leq A_i \leq 10^9$
- The sum of $N$ across all test cases does not exceed $10^5$
content.inputFormat:
- The first line of input will contain a single integer $T$, denoting the number of test cases. The description of the test cases follows.
- The first line of each test case contains a single integer $N$, denoting the length of array $A$.
- The second line of each test case contains $N$ space-separated integers $A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_N$ — denoting the array $A$.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output on a new line the maximum value of $A_1+A_N$ you can get after several right rotations.
